planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in early spring or fall; in zone 9 plant September–March. Ice Dance establishes quickly and begins spreading slowly by rhizomes to form a dense, weed-suppressing ground cover. It performs best in consistent shade — full sun bleaches the foliage and causes summer decline in zones 8–9. Keep consistently moist during establishment, then water moderate amounts; once established it tolerates periods of dryness under shade trees better than most sedges. Divide every 3–4 years to maintain vigor and prevent the center from dying out.

directions: Plant in shade to deep shade in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Ice Dance Japanese Sedge strongly prefers consistent shade and declines or bleaches in full sun, especially in the South; Ice Dance is a variegated cultivar of Japanese sedge (Carex morrowii) producing arching, strap-like leaves with a bold white margin on dark green centers — one of the most striking foliage combinations available in shade-tolerant ground covers. The clean white-and-green variegation creates a luminous, refined effect under trees and in shaded borders, brightening areas where most plants produce only undifferentiated green foliage. Unlike many variegated plants where the color pattern is subtle, Ice Dance's contrast between bright white margins and deep green centers is bold and distinctive enough to read clearly even in low-light conditions — making it effective as a design element even in deep shade situations. The evergreen to semi-evergreen habit in zone 9 provides year-round foliage interest, making Ice Dance one of the most practically valuable shade ground covers available for Gulf South landscapes. The slowly spreading rhizomes form a dense, low-maintenance colony that suppresses weeds effectively under established trees — one of the most challenging landscape conditions to manage successfully.
sources: North Carolina State University Extension — Carex morrowii 'Ice Dance' Plant Profile
University of Georgia Extension — Ornamental Sedges for Southern Shade Gardens
Missouri Botanical Garden — Carex morrowii Cultivar Guide

planting.note: Plant container-grown palms in spring after temperatures stabilize; in zone 9 plant March–May. Chamaedorea radicalis is among the most cold-hardy of the Chamaedorea palms, tolerating temperatures to about 18–22°F when established — brief dips into the upper teens may cause frond damage without killing established plants. It is extremely slow-growing in cool conditions but accelerates in warm, humid summers. Plant in a sheltered location with northern or eastern shade exposure; deep root establishment takes 1–2 years before visible trunk growth begins. Mature plants produce small clusters of orange to red fruits that attract birds.

directions: Plant in shade to partial shade in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Chamaedorea radicalis is a forest understory palm that thrives in the same deep shade conditions that exclude most palms; Radicalis Palm is a small, slow-growing Mexican understory palm that is exceptional among palms for its genuine deep shade tolerance — not merely surviving in shade as most palms will, but genuinely thriving and looking its best in the dappled to dense shade beneath established trees. The plant grows as a low, clumping palm with arching, pinnate fronds of medium-to-dark green leaflets, creating a refined, tropical texture in shaded garden areas at a manageable scale of 3–5 feet. While most landscape palms are unambiguously full-sun plants, Chamaedorea radicalis fills the specific niche of a palm-textured shade plant — providing the tropical character of a palm frond in the shaded woodland garden conditions where conventional palms fail. For zone 9 gardeners working with established oak or magnolia canopy, Chamaedorea radicalis is one of the very few palms that can be successfully incorporated into the understory without the decline and eventual death that shade causes in Queen palms, Windmill palms, and most other landscape palms.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Chamaedorea Palms for Florida Landscapes
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Cold-Hardy Palms for Gulf Coast
International Palm Society — Chamaedorea radicalis Cold Hardiness

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring or fall; in zone 9 plant February–April or September–November. Sugar Shack is a compact selection reaching only 4–5 feet, far smaller than the 8–12 foot species; it fits into residential landscapes where the full-sized species would be too large. Buttonbush is a wetland native that grows along stream banks, pond edges, and in low-lying areas with poor drainage — it is one of the very few ornamental shrubs suitable for consistently wet or periodically flooded sites. Blooms on new wood; prune in late winter to control size and stimulate maximum flowering.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in moist to wet, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Sugar Shack Buttonbush thrives in the poorly drained, wet soils that defeat most landscape shrubs and is actually native to swamp and wetland margins; Sugar Shack is a compact cultivar of native Buttonbush (Cephalanthus occidentalis), introduced by Bailey Nurseries as a landscape-appropriate size selection of one of the most ecologically valuable native shrubs in North America. The extraordinary round, spherical, 1–1.5 inch white flower heads that appear in July–August are genuinely unusual — the flower ball is densely packed with tiny, long-tubed white florets with projecting styles that give the whole ball a pin-cushion or sputnik-like radiating texture unlike any other common landscape plant. This unusual flower structure is extremely attractive to butterflies (especially swallowtails and skippers), bumblebees, and other nectar-seeking pollinators — making Buttonbush one of the highest-value native pollinator plants available. The subsequent seed heads remain through winter as small, persistent brown balls that provide food for ducks, songbirds, and small mammals. As a native species adapted to Gulf Coast wetlands, Sugar Shack Buttonbush fills the specific niche of a flowering shrub for wet, low-lying sites that are often the most challenging spots in residential landscapes.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Cephalanthus occidentalis Native Plant
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Buttonbush for Florida Native Landscapes
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Native Shrubs for Wet Sites in Gulf South

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring or fall; in zone 9 plant September–March. Sweet Kate goes semi-dormant in the heat of mid-summer, dying back to the ground — cut back the tired foliage in July and it will resprout fresh golden foliage in fall. The bright yellow-gold foliage color is most vivid in spring and fall when temperatures are moderate; some green may develop in summer heat. Divide every 2–3 years when clumps become crowded. Partial shade maintains the golden leaf color better than full sun and prevents the leaf scorching that can occur on the thin, golden foliage in extreme summer heat.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Sweet Kate performs best with morning sun and afternoon shade, which maintains the golden foliage color and prevents summer leaf scorch; Sweet Kate is a spiderwort cultivar valued primarily for its extraordinary bright golden-yellow foliage rather than its flowers, creating an unusual color combination when the vivid blue-violet, three-petaled spiderwort flowers open against the luminous gold-yellow leaf background. The foliage color — a genuine, clear, clean golden-yellow rather than the chartreuse-green or lime-yellow of many 'gold' plants — provides exceptional contrast potential with dark-foliaged companions (black-eyed Susan, dark-leaved coral bells) and with blue-flowered plants. The flowers themselves, like all Tradescantia, are produced in succession — each individual flower lasts only a single morning before collapsing, but new buds continue opening from the same cluster over weeks, maintaining the blue-and-gold display from late spring through early summer. Sweet Kate's combination of unusual leaf color and blue flower-against-gold-foliage display makes it one of the most visually distinctive herbaceous perennials for spring and fall interest in partial shade.
sources: North Carolina State University Extension — Tradescantia 'Sweet Kate' Cultivar Profile
University of Georgia Extension — Spiderwort Varieties for Southern Gardens
Missouri Botanical Garden — Tradescantia cultivar database

planting.note: Direct sow after all frost danger has passed in full sun; in zone 9 plant March–June. PKM-1 grows explosively fast in warm weather — seedlings can reach 8–10 feet in a single season. Moringa is killed to the ground by frost but the roots survive in zone 9 to resprout vigorously in spring. For continuous leaf harvest, cut plants back to 3–4 feet when they reach 6–8 feet to stimulate dense, bushy branching with easily reachable leaves. Seeds germinate without treatment but soaking overnight speeds germination. PKM-1 was developed by Periyakulam Horticultural College and Research Institute in Tamil Nadu and is one of the most productive pod-bearing selections available.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil — moringa is extremely drought tolerant once established and is one of the few productive food trees that thrives in poor, dry, neglected sites; PKM-1 is an improved selection of Moringa (Moringa oleifera, the 'miracle tree') developed specifically for high pod yield and early production — bearing its first edible pods within 6–8 months of sowing from seed compared to the 12–18 months typical of seedling-grown moringa. The leaves, young pods (drumsticks), and flowers of moringa are all edible and among the most nutritionally dense plant foods available — the leaves contain protein, vitamin C, calcium, iron, and a range of antioxidants at concentrations that have made moringa a focus of nutritional research and a widely promoted food security crop. In zone 9 moringa grows as a rapidly resprouting tender perennial — killed to the ground by frost but regenerating vigorously from the root in spring and often reaching 10–15 feet before the next frost. The soft, ferny, compound leaves are harvested continuously throughout the growing season by cutting branch tips, and the long, angular seed pods (the 'drumsticks' of Indian cuisine) are harvested when young and tender for cooking in curries, soups, and stir-fries.
sources: Tamil Nadu Agricultural University — PKM-1 Moringa Variety Description
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Moringa oleifera for Florida
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Moringa Production for Texas Gulf Coast
